kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 20-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall), now the latest PUBLISHED official at this ~06:05 London refresh on Wed 22-Jul (Westmetall had not yet posted the 21-Jul settlement; LME officials print 13:20 London and republish with a lag). +$2.00 (+0.06%) vs 17-Jul. Backdrop escalated further: the US flew an 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran, Iran hit another tanker in the Strait of Hormuz early Tuesday, and Yemen's Houthis declared a maritime embargo on Saudi Arabia (a potential new front) - even as mediators floated a 10-day ceasefire. The 21-Jul session itself traded firmer, ~$3,167/t (+0.93%, Trading Economics).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 20-Jul official, +$1.00 (+0.03%) vs 17-Jul. Resistance $3,230 (MA20) then $3,260 (.382 retracement of the Jun-Jul decline); support the early-July four-month low near $3,085. The 21-Jul traded session pushed back above $3,160.

kpi_cards.label: CASH-3M ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: BACKWARDATION widened to +$8.50 (cash $3,156.00 over 3M $3,147.50) on the 20-Jul official, up from +$7.50. Positive cash-3M = backwardation = prompt tightness, consistent with LME stock at 279,775 t, the lowest since 2022. Watch whether the fresh tanker attack and Houthi embargo threat steepen the front at the next official.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: -325 t on the 20-Jul official; roughly -45.1% YTD from 509,250 t on 02-Jan-2026. Below 280,000 t and the lowest since 2022, still drawing. SHFE stocks are falling in parallel - a genuinely tight physical picture on both sides of the arb.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: 20-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall) $3,156.00 vs 17-Jul $3,154.00 = +$2.00 (+0.06%). This is the LATEST PUBLISHED official at the ~06:05 London refresh on Wed 22-Jul; Westmetall had not yet posted the 21-Jul official (LME officials print 13:20 London and republish with a lag). The 21-Jul session separately traded firmer - see the metals board. avg = June-2026 monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 20-Jul official $3,147.50 vs 17-Jul $3,146.50 (+$1.00, +0.03%) - latest published official. Resistance stack: $3,230 (MA20), then $3,260 (.382 of the June-July decline). avg = June-2026 3M mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash-3M spread
benchmark.note: BACKWARDATION at +$8.50 (cash $3,156.00 over 3M $3,147.50) on the 20-Jul official, wider than the 17-Jul +$7.50. Positive cash-3M = backwardation = prompt tightness, consistent with LME stock at 279,775 t, the lowest since 2022. The next official is the first read on whether the fresh tanker attack widens it further.

benchmark.name: LME stock (t)
benchmark.note: 279,775 t at the 20-Jul official, down 325 t on the day and roughly -45.1% year-to-date from 509,250 t on 02-Jan-2026. Below 280,000 t and the lowest since 2022. Draws have been continuous through July; SHFE stockpiles are also declining.

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t (S&P Global Platts, assessed 06-Jul-2026) - a decade high, roughly +12% q/q vs Q2's $350-353/t. Q4 negotiations open around September against this anchor; the swing variable is whether Hormuz disruption persists at current intensity. Source: Platts via IndexBox. avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot (MJP spot)
premiums.src: Spot firming toward the Q3 settlement: Platts CIF Japan spot last publicly at ~$370/t (06-Jul); SMM's 'squeeze risk climbs under LME deep backwardation' analysis flags tight spot supply pushing the QMJP premium up. Point ~$365/t. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343 / SMM weekly.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: Platts P1020A duty-UNPAID in-warehouse Rotterdam assessed $525-560/t (mid $542.50) on 03-Jul-2026, down from $600-625/t on 13-May as the Middle East war premium faded. No fresher public print. Structural floor: 6% EU duty plus CBAM plus idled European capacity. Liquidity thin - Rotterdam competes with Asia for units.

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added 6063 billet conversion premium, in-whs duty-paid Rotterdam — last public reference (~late-Jun; no fresh public print). Conversion premia are stickier than ingot premia; Mozal remains down. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0002.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Platts US MW Transaction premium last publicly assessed 108.7¢/lb ≈ $2,396/t (03-Jul). The all-in US delivered price embeds the 50% Section-232 tariff (≈$1,577/t at the 17-Jul cash of $3,154), so the policy leg dominates. Alcoa's Q2 flagged Section-232 on Canadian metal rising ~$35m q/q. Verify S&P Global.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: SMM weekly DDP print 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul). Duty-paid = LME + Section-232 tariff (50%) + market premium; the policy leg is ~two-thirds of the all-in. The tariff-$ component eased ~$8/t with Friday's softer LME. Verify Platts/Fastmarkets.

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Firmed on the escalation - spiking toward $91/bbl on 21-Jul (+2.1%) as the US flew an 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran and another tanker was hit in the Strait of Hormuz - before easing back toward $88-90. Energy is the largest smelter cost line; European and Asian smelters carry the most exposure through the gas channel.

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: DXY ~100.96, little changed and holding firm amid the US-Iran conflict. A steady-to-softer dollar keeps a modest tailwind under dollar-priced metals even as the geopolitical risk premium re-widens.

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Euro ~1.1414, holding near recent levels. The ECB still carries a war-energy inflation problem with Brent near $90 and European gas (TTF ~EUR 58/MWh) exposed to any further Gulf escalation.

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Yuan steady ~6.764. China's Q2 GDP grew 4.3% y/y (below the 4.5% consensus, weakest since Q4-2022); June exports of unwrought aluminium hit a record 711 kt (H1 +16.3% y/y at 3.4 Mt) while primary-metal imports fell ~20% m/m - production/export strength against weak domestic demand, keeping the record export valve open.

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen ~162.5, little changed. A still-weak yen cushions Japan-bound cargo premia in local terms; MJP Q4 talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high $395 anchor and an escalating - but possibly negotiable - Gulf conflict.

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: 10Y ~4.60%, little changed. Real rates remain the macro cap on metals; the swing risk this week is geopolitical (oil-driven inflation pass-through and the 10-day ceasefire proposal) rather than the Fed.

macro_events: STRIKES TO AN 11TH NIGHT: The US military completed an 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran as attacks overshadowed diplomacy. The interim ceasefire framed by the 14-June memorandum of understanding (intended to end the conflict within 60 days) collapsed in July after Iran struck commercial vessels that bypassed its preapproved Hormuz route. (Washington Post; Britannica)

FRESH TANKER ATTACK + NEW FRONT: Iran attacked a tanker in the Strait of Hormuz early Tuesday 21-Jul, and Yemen's Houthis declared a maritime embargo / naval blockade against Saudi Arabia - potentially opening a new front. The US has moved to blockade Iranian ports as Washington seeks control of the strait. The Gulf accounts for roughly 9% of global primary aluminium supply and Hormuz traffic is largely stalled. (CBSNews; CNBC)

10-DAY CEASEFIRE ON THE TABLE: Regional mediators presented Washington and Tehran with a proposal for a 10-day ceasefire, but large divisions remain and President Trump said the US would retaliate following the deaths of several American troops - a genuinely two-sided catalyst (de-escalation vs. further escalation). (CNBC)
ENERGY: Brent firmed toward $90-91/bbl. Sustained crude and gas strength raises smelter operating costs and biases marginal European and Asian capacity toward curtailment rather than restart.

ALUMINA RELIEF RAMPING: EGA's Al Taweelah refinery has been at full alumina production since 10-Jul, targeting 50% of plant capacity within days of restart and full capability by end-2026. This is the principal counterweight to the geopolitical supply risk and helps explain the flat price ranging rather than spiking. (EGA)

US TRADE POLICY: The Section 232 aluminium tariff remains at 50% and now applies to the FULL customs value of covered articles and derivatives (effective 06-Apr-2026), per the April-2026 White House proclamation. This is the single largest administered component of the US duty-paid premium. (White House; CRS)
lme_commentary: The 20-Jul official is the latest PUBLISHED LME official (cash $3,156.00 / 3M $3,147.50 / stock 279,775 t): Westmetall had not yet posted the 21-Jul settlement at this ~06:05 London refresh on Wed 22-Jul, and LME officials print at 13:20 London before being republished with a lag. Notably the 20-Jul official ticked slightly HIGHER (+$2.00 cash), and the 21-Jul traded session firmed further to about $3,167/t (+0.93%, Trading Economics), holding the rebound off the early-July four-month low near $3,085. Copper firmed in parallel - the 20-Jul LME 3M official was $13,616.00, up on the week. The geopolitical tape escalated again: the US flew an 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran, Iran hit another tanker in the Strait of Hormuz early Tuesday, and Yemen's Houthis declared a maritime embargo on Saudi Arabia - a potential new front - while the US moved to blockade Iranian ports. The two-sided wrinkle is a 10-day ceasefire proposal now in front of Washington and Tehran, though large divisions remain. The counterweight on supply is real: EGA's Al Taweelah alumina refinery has been at full production since 10-Jul and is ramping toward full capability by end-2026, and Chinese exports hit a record 711 kt in June. Structurally the market stays tight - LME stock at 279,775 t is the lowest since 2022 and roughly 45% below the start of the year, SHFE stocks are drawing, and the cash-3M curve backwardation widened to +$8.50. The next official print is the first clean read on whether the fresh tanker attack and the Houthi threat steepen the front of the curve.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: $328/t FOB Western Australia (SMM, 09-Jul) — down from ~$338 as the EGA Al Taweelah refinery restart (10-Jul) ramps and the ex-China surplus deepens. The SMM Alumina Index eased to ¥2,730.17/t (−¥12.8 w/w). Gulf-routed cargoes still carry elevated war-risk cover given the further-escalated Kuwaiti energy-infrastructure strikes. Verify SMM/Fastmarkets.

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region ~$303/t — easing on the building surplus (Q1 avg $306.9; latest public reference, no fresher print surfaced). SMM flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June — a partial offset to the EGA restart. Verify Platts.

alumina.name: SHFE-region alumina (SMM Alumina Index)
alumina.src: SMM Alumina Index ¥2,730.17/t, down ¥12.8/t week-on-week; regional spot Shandong ¥2,710–2,780, Henan ¥2,730–2,800, Shanxi ¥2,750–2,800, Guangxi ¥2,600–2,680, Guizhou ¥2,760–2,800. Futures trade below moving averages with port stocks building even as the Gulf strait squeeze still weighs on regional refinery feed (~60% of Gulf alumina imports transit Hormuz).

alumina.name: Platts FOB Australia (futures)
alumina.src: Alumina FOB Australia (Platts) futures ~$309.4/t (-0.5% on the day, CME/TradingView, 21-Jul) - easing on the building ex-China surplus as the EGA restart ramps.

alumina_notes: The alumina market remains the mirror image of metal: globally LOOSENING (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa restart ramping since 10-Jul, Guinea bauxite oversupply, Chinese port stocks building; SMM Alumina Index down to ¥2,730.17/t, −¥12.8 w/w) — ex-China consolidation has stepped down toward the $300–330 band (FOB Australia now $328, from ~$338).

The regional wrinkle has widened further overnight: a 9th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran, a second consecutive Iranian strike on Kuwait's Shuaiba desalination/power complex, and Strait transits down to as few as ~3 vessels/24h all re-price the war-risk cover embedded in the ~60% of Gulf alumina imports that transit Hormuz. SMM separately flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June.

Guinea's pending export-quota policy (a considered cap near 150 Mt for 2026, vs 178 Mt shipped in 2025, targeting >$100/dmt bauxite) remains undecreed as of end-June and is the one clear bullish catalyst on the commodity leg; record Australian and Metro Mining shipments underline how long the seaborne bauxite market currently is.
net_read: TIGHT MARKET, TWO-SIDED CATALYST. The flat price is firming, not spiking: the 20-Jul official rose $2 to $3,156.00 and the 21-Jul session traded to about $3,167/t (+0.93%). Escalation continues - an 11th night of US strikes, a fresh Hormuz tanker attack, and a Houthi embargo threat against Saudi shipping keep roughly 9% of global primary supply at risk with strait traffic stalled - but two forces cap the upside: EGA's Al Taweelah alumina restart (full production since 10-Jul, ramping to full capability by end-2026) and record Chinese exports (June 711 kt). The new variable is a 10-day ceasefire proposal on the table, which cuts both ways. The structural picture has not loosened: LME stock is 279,775 t, the lowest since 2022 and ~-45% YTD, SHFE inventories are falling, and cash-3M holds a +$8.50 backwardation. Brent near $90/bbl keeps smelter power costs pointed the wrong way. The asymmetry still favours the upside on any interruption to the Al Taweelah ramp or any widening of the tanker attacks and the Houthi blockade into general aluminium-bearing traffic; the offsetting downside trigger is a durable ceasefire.

feed.text: FRESH TANKER ATTACK: Iran attacked a tanker in the Strait of Hormuz early Tuesday, the latest in a run of attacks that has largely stalled strait traffic. Roughly 80-85% of Gulf aluminium exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports use this waterway (CBSNews; CNBC).

NEW FRONT - HOUTHI EMBARGO: Yemen's Iran-backed Houthis declared a maritime embargo / naval blockade against Saudi Arabia, threatening to open a new front and widen the shipping-risk zone beyond Hormuz (CNBC).

STRIKES TO 11TH NIGHT: The US completed an 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran; the US has moved to blockade Iranian ports as Washington seeks control of the Strait of Hormuz (Washington Post; CBSNews).

CEASEFIRE PROPOSAL: Regional mediators presented Washington and Tehran with a 10-day ceasefire proposal - a potential de-escalation path, though large divisions remain and the June MoU already collapsed once (CNBC).

LME OFFICIAL FIRMER: The 20-Jul official ticked up to cash $3,156.00 / 3M $3,147.50 (stock 279,775 t); the cash-3M backwardation widened to +$8.50. The 21-Jul session traded to ~$3,167/t (+0.93%) (Westmetall; Trading Economics).

SUPPLY RELIEF: EGA resumed full alumina production at Al Taweelah on 10-Jul after a three-and-a-half-month outage, targeting 50% of plant capacity within days and full capability by end-2026. The refinery produced 2.4 Mt of alumina in 2025 (EGA).

news.headline: The US-Iran conflict entered an 11th night of strikes and Iran hit another tanker in the Strait of Hormuz early Tuesday, while Yemen's Houthis declared a maritime embargo against Saudi Arabia - a potential new front. The US is moving to blockade Iranian ports as Washington seeks control of the strait. Hormuz traffic is largely stalled.

Regional mediators presented Washington and Tehran with a proposal for a 10-day ceasefire. Large divisions remain and the June-14 memorandum of understanding already collapsed once in July, but a durable truce would release the war-risk premium and reopen Gulf shipping - the key two-sided catalyst for the flat price.

EGA's Al Taweelah refinery has been at full alumina production since 10-Jul, targeting 50% of capacity within days and full capability by end-2026. At 2.4 Mt in 2025 it supplies close to half of EGA's alumina need - the only material supply-side relief currently visible and a key reason the flat price is ranging rather than spiking.

LME stock fell to 279,775 t on the 20-Jul official, the lowest since 2022 and roughly 45% below the 509,250 t start-of-year level, with SHFE stockpiles also declining. Cash-3M widened to a +$8.50 backwardation - prompt tightness, not surplus.

Norsk Hydro released Q2 2026 results this morning (22-Jul, 07:00 CEST) - the first European producer P&L covering a full quarter of the disruption regime and the cleanest public read on European smelter power costs and regional premium pass-through. Guidance into the quarter had pointed to adjusted EBITDA around NOK 8.7-9.0bn.

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.97bn (+24% q/q, highest in nearly a decade); adj. EPS $2.12 (MISS vs ~$2.31 consensus); adj. net income $562m; adj. EBITDA $901m (from $595m in Q1); Aluminum segment RECORD EBITDA $1.073bn at 32.3% margin.
earnings.readthrough: First producer P&L under the Hormuz-disruption regime: record aluminium margins and near-decade-high revenue on higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums, but adj. EPS missed on higher costs — notably ~$35m more Section-232 tariff on Canadian metal into the US — and the shares fell. South32 upstream acquisition ($4.1bn upfront, up to $5.6bn contingent to 2030) progressing, close expected H1-2027. The tariff is a double edge: it inflates US premia but taxes Alcoa's cross-border Canadian metal.

earnings.headline: Q2 2026 results released this morning (22-Jul, 07:00 CEST). Guidance into the quarter pointed to adjusted EBITDA ~NOK 8.7-9.0bn, with ~67% of Q2 primary output hedged near $3,000/t and realised premiums guided at ~$530-580/t. Final reported figures were still being digested at this ~06:05 London refresh.
earnings.readthrough: First European producer P&L covering a full quarter of the Hormuz-disruption regime - the read on Norwegian/European smelter power costs (Brent near $90, TTF gas ~EUR 58/MWh) and regional premium realisation. Hedging near $3,000/t caps the upside capture vs a spot LME above $3,150.

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: H1 copper-equivalent output +3% y/y; aluminium division improved; strong bauxite recovery; FY aluminium guidance UNCHANGED at 3.25–3.45 Mt; Pilbara Q2 shipments 89 Mt (+5%).
earnings.readthrough: First upstream print of the Q2 season (this morning): Western volumes are intact — the Gulf disruption is expressing itself in prices, freight and premia rather than output. Kitimat/AP60 ramp continues; H1 financial results due 13-Aug.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings.headline: Guides H1-26 net profit ¥1.8–1.95bn, +154.4–175.6% YoY.
earnings.readthrough: Credits industry-chain cost reduction, aluminium prices fluctuating at highs, high-value-added deep-processing mix and energy-saving retrofits — the first sizeable China H1 pre-announcement of the season and a positive read-across for Chinese producer margins.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly and episodic; entries show each listed peer's most recent reported results as of 22-Jul (public, listed-company information). NEW: Norsk Hydro released Q2 2026 this morning (22-Jul, 07:00 CEST) - the first European producer P&L of the disruption quarter; guidance had pointed to adjusted EBITDA ~NOK 8.7-9.0bn with ~67% of primary output hedged near $3,000/t. Full reported figures were still being digested at this ~06:05 London refresh. Prior season: Alcoa (16-Jul) posted record Aluminum-segment EBITDA ($1.073bn, 32.3% margin) and near-decade-high revenue ($3.97bn) but missed on adjusted EPS ($2.12 vs ~$2.31) on ~$35m of extra Section-232 tariff on Canadian metal; Rio Tinto's 15-Jul production report held aluminium guidance at 3.25-3.45 Mt.

commercial.text: The flat price is firming: the 20-Jul official rose $2 to cash $3,156.00 / 3M $3,147.50 and the 21-Jul session traded to about $3,167/t (+0.93%). Monday's 11th-night strikes and Tuesday's tanker attack are not yet in a settled official. Resistance $3,230 (MA20) then $3,260; support the early-July low near $3,085.

Cash-3M backwardation widened to +$8.50 with LME stock at 279,775 t, below 280k, the lowest since 2022 and roughly -45% YTD, and SHFE stocks also drawing. Prompt tightness is genuine on both sides of the arb. The next official is the first clean read on whether the fresh tanker attack and the Houthi threat steepen the front.

The three regions remain decoupled: Japan's Q3 MJP settled at an 11-year-high $395/t on scarcity, Rotterdam duty-unpaid has faded to a $542.50/t mid as the war premium receded toward a structural 6%-duty-plus-CBAM floor, and the US duty-paid premium is dominated by the 50% Section 232 rate - roughly $1,578/t of administered cost at the 20-Jul cash. Watch Q4 MJP talks opening around September.

Brent near $90/bbl and TTF gas ~EUR 58/MWh keep the largest smelter cost line pointed the wrong way, with European and Asian producers most exposed. On the other side, EGA's Al Taweelah alumina ramp and record Chinese exports are the main reasons the flat price is not spiking.

Norsk Hydro Q2 out this morning (22-Jul). Beyond that: whether the 10-day ceasefire proposal gains traction or collapses like the June MoU; the pace of the Al Taweelah ramp; whether the Houthi embargo materialises into actual Saudi-lane disruption; and whether LME stock keeps drawing below 279,775 t.

bottom_line: On 22-Jul the benchmark table carries the 20-Jul LME official (cash $3,156.00, 3M $3,147.50, cash-3M +$8.50 backwardation, stock 279,775 t, ~-45% YTD) - the latest published, as Westmetall had not posted the 21-Jul settlement at this ~06:05 London refresh. Both the official (+$2) and the 21-Jul traded session (~$3,167/t, +0.93%) firmed. The geopolitical backdrop escalated again - an 11th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran, a fresh tanker attack in the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, a Houthi maritime-embargo threat against Saudi Arabia, and a US move to blockade Iranian ports - but mediators have put a 10-day ceasefire proposal in front of both sides, a genuinely two-sided catalyst. The offsetting supply force is EGA's Al Taweelah alumina refinery (full production since 10-Jul, ramping to full capability by end-2026) plus record Chinese exports (June 711 kt). Net: a tight physical market with the lowest LME stocks since 2022 and a widening backwardation, temporarily balanced by visible alumina relief and heavy Chinese supply - an equilibrium that a ceasefire or a supply interruption could break in either direction. Norsk Hydro's Q2 results, out this morning (22-Jul, 07:00 CEST), are the key scheduled read.

caveats: Report refreshed Wed 22-Jul-2026, ~06:05 London - BEFORE today's LME ring (official settlement 13:20 London). Westmetall had not yet published the 21-Jul official at refresh time, so the benchmark table carries the 20-Jul official (cash $3,156.00 / 3M $3,147.50 / stock 279,775 t). This is a publication-timing constraint, not a stale copy: the 21-Jul session is captured separately in the metals board using the publicly reported traded close (~$3,167/t).

MIXED SOURCE BASIS ON THE BOARD: aluminium, nickel, zinc and lead levels on the metals board are publicly reported traded prices (Trading Economics), while copper uses the LME 20-Jul 3-month official (Westmetall). LME OFFICIAL settlements (the benchmark table and lme_series) come from Westmetall. Official and traded prices are different reference points and will not tie out exactly.

GEOPOLITICAL DEVELOPMENTS ARE FAST-MOVING and are NOT yet reflected in any settled LME official. The 11th-night strikes, Tuesday's tanker attack, the Houthi embargo threat and the 10-day ceasefire proposal all post-date the 20-Jul official. Treat the flat price as lagging the news flow, and note the ceasefire proposal makes the near-term skew two-sided.

PREMIUM ASSESSMENTS ARE PERIODIC, NOT DAILY: MJP settles quarterly, Rotterdam duty-unpaid and US Midwest are assessed by Platts/Fastmarkets on their own schedules. The figures shown are the latest PUBLIC prints (Rotterdam duty-unpaid mid $542.50/t and US Midwest 108.7 c/lb from the 03-Jul assessments; MJP Q3 settled $395/t on 06-Jul). They are not daily marks.

ALL CONTENT IS PUBLIC MARKET INFORMATION for situational awareness only. No confidential, internal, company-specific or position data is included, and nothing here is trading, hedging or investment advice. Elliott Wave analysis is technical context, not a forecast or recommendation.

forward_path.basis: Latest PUBLISHED official (Westmetall); +$2.00 vs 17-Jul. Cash-3M +$8.50 BACKWARDATION. Next official today, 22-Jul, 13:20 London.
Latest published official; +$1.00 vs 17-Jul; front spread +$8.50 BACKWARDATION. Resistance $3,230 (MA20) then $3,260 (.382). The 21-Jul traded session firmed to ~$3,167/t.

outlook.curve_note: The curve firmed and the backwardation widened. The cash-3M spread was last published at +$8.50 BACKWARDATION on the 20-Jul official (cash $3,156.00 over 3M $3,147.50), up from +$7.50, on a 279,775 t stock base (-45.1% YTD, below 280k for the first time this cycle). The disruption the spread is pricing escalated again - an 11th consecutive night of US strikes, a fresh Hormuz tanker attack, a Houthi embargo threat against Saudi shipping, and a US move to blockade Iranian ports - but mediators have tabled a 10-day ceasefire, so the near-term skew is two-sided rather than one-way. The counterweight is still quantified at record scale: China exported 711 kt in June (H1 +16.3%) into a Q2 GDP print that missed at 4.3%, and EGA's Al Taweelah alumina restart keeps ramping. The 2026 consensus band ($3,200-3,500 averages) and the ~-365 kt structural deficit remain the floor beneath the argument; today's 13:20 London ring is the first chance for the curve to reprice the accumulated news.

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base
First structural deficit in ~20 years; BofA flags near-term downside

balance.label: BofA/Morgan/BNP structural deficit; Goldman flags later surplus risk

capacity_pipeline.change: Alumina refinery restarted 10-Jul (ramping through H2, full capability targeted end-2026); smelter rebuild ahead of schedule
~19% capacity in controlled shutdown through the Gulf disruption; restart path improves as US–Iran diplomacy advances

Output near the 45 Mt self-imposed cap; June unwrought-aluminium exports a RECORD 711 kt (+12.5% m/m; H1 3.4 Mt, +16.3% y/y); primary-metal imports down ~20% m/m; Q2 GDP 4.3% - production strong, domestic demand weak
capacity_pipeline.timing: Structural ceiling on output; record export flow

capacity_pipeline.change: New smelter capacity ramping (BofA flags as added supply)
Q2 production report (15-Jul): H1 CuEq +3%, aluminium guidance intact 3.25–3.45 Mt, bauxite recovering; AP60/Kitimat incremental low-carbon capacity

scenarios.drivers: Hormuz enforcement widens from crude tankers into general merchant traffic, or the Al Taweelah ramp stalls - either puts physical units at risk into a market with the lowest LME stocks since 2022 and no inventory buffer
drivers_list: Enforcement escalation: Iran disabled two tankers on 20-Jul; extension to general merchant traffic would expose roughly 9% of global primary supply
No inventory cushion: LME stock 280,100 t (lowest since 2022, about -45% YTD), SHFE drawing, cash-3M in backwardation
Energy: Brent above $90/bbl raises marginal costs and biases European/Asian capacity toward curtailment

scenarios.drivers: The standoff persists - escalation at Hormuz offset by a steady Al Taweelah alumina ramp and record Chinese exports - or a partial ceasefire holds, leaving the flat price ranging between the early-July low near $3,085 and the $3,230-3,260 resistance stack
drivers_list: Al Taweelah ramps to 50% and beyond on schedule, capping alumina-driven cost pressure
Interdictions stay focused on crude rather than general cargo, keeping aluminium flows degraded but not halted
Backwardation persists but does not gap - prompt tight, forward balanced

scenarios.drivers: A durable 10-day ceasefire reopens the strait while the Al Taweelah ramp completes ahead of schedule, releasing both the war premium and the alumina cost pressure into softening, high-price-rationed demand
drivers_list: The 10-day ceasefire proposal firms into a durable truce and shipping normalises, collapsing the war-risk premium
Alumina relief arrives faster than expected as Al Taweelah reaches full capability early; record Chinese exports keep flowing
Demand destruction from sustained high prices plus the 50% Section 232 rate on US imports

risks.risk: Hormuz / Gulf escalation: an 11th night of US strikes, a fresh tanker attack on 21-Jul, and a Houthi maritime-embargo threat against Saudi Arabia keep roughly 9% of global primary aluminium supply at risk with strait traffic largely stalled; a widening into general aluminium-bearing traffic would put physical units directly at risk
Inventory floor: LME stock at 279,775 t is the lowest since 2022 and roughly -45% YTD with SHFE also drawing - there is no inventory buffer to absorb a fresh supply interruption
Energy cost squeeze: Brent above $90/bbl with EU gas following on a lag compresses marginal-producer margins in Europe and Asia and biases capacity toward curtailment rather than restart
Al Taweelah ramp disappoints or is interrupted - the alumina relief is the principal offset holding the flat price down and it is a ramp toward end-2026, not delivered tonnage
Demand destruction: sustained elevated prices plus the 50% Section 232 rate on US imports encourage substitution and scrap displacement, particularly in packaging and construction

Two-sided diplomacy: a durable 10-day ceasefire would reopen Gulf shipping and collapse the war-risk premium sharply (downside to price); a collapse like the June MoU would re-escalate. A Section 232 rate change would likewise reprice the US premium complex quickly

catalysts.event: Norsk Hydro Q2 2026 results (released this morning; webcast 08:30 CEST) - first European producer P&L covering a full quarter of the disruption regime; read on European smelter power costs and regional premium pass-through
Next LME official ring - the first settled print able to reflect the 11th-night strikes and Tuesday's tanker attack; watch whether the cash-3M backwardation steepens beyond +$8.50
LME warehouse stock report - whether draws continue below 280,100 t, the lowest level since 2022
EGA Al Taweelah ramp from 50% of capacity toward full capability by end-2026 - the principal supply-side offset in the current balance
Q4-2026 MJP CIF Japan premium negotiations open against the decade-high Q3 settlement of $395/t

The 10-day ceasefire proposal in front of Washington and Tehran - traction would reopen Gulf shipping and release the war premium; collapse would re-escalate. The single biggest two-sided swing factor

ai_analysis: The defining feature this session is resilience: the flat price firmed - the 20-Jul official rose $2 to $3,156.00 and the 21-Jul session traded to about $3,167/t (+0.93%) - even as the conflict escalated to an 11th night of strikes, a fresh Hormuz tanker attack, and a Houthi embargo threat against Saudi Arabia. A market that holds and grinds higher into that tape is signalling that physical tightness dominates positioning.

The structural tightness has not eased: LME stock at 279,775 t is the lowest since 2022 and roughly 45% below the start of the year, SHFE inventories are drawing, and cash-3M widened to a +$8.50 backwardation. A market with those characteristics has no inventory to absorb a fresh interruption. The offsets are EGA's Al Taweelah alumina ramp and record Chinese exports (June 711 kt) - real, but the alumina relief is a ramp toward end-2026, not delivered tonnage.

The new variable is the 10-day ceasefire proposal now in front of Washington and Tehran. It makes the near-term skew genuinely two-sided: a durable truce reopens Gulf shipping and releases the war-risk premium (downside), while a collapse like the June MoU re-escalates into a market with no inventory buffer (upside). The Houthi embargo threat is the tail risk that would widen the disruption zone beyond Hormuz.

Regionally the premium complex stays decoupled and should be read separately: Japan's Q3 MJP settled at an 11-year-high $395/t on scarcity, Rotterdam duty-unpaid has faded to $542.50/t toward a structural 6%-duty-plus-CBAM floor, and the US duty-paid premium is dominated by the administered 50% Section 232 rate (~$1,578/t at the 20-Jul cash). Norsk Hydro's Q2, out this morning, is the next public read on how these flow into producer margins.

premium_explainer.desc: The physical-delivery adder over the LME price: regional logistics, duties/tariffs and local tightness — quoted $/t (or ¢/lb in the US) on top of LME cash. It is what converts an exchange price into a delivered price.

Q3-2026 settled at $395/t CIF Japan (Platts, 06-Jul) — a decade high and the fourth straight rise ($86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395). SMM flags spot tightness and squeeze risk under the LME's deep backwardation, firming CIF spot toward ~$365. Q4 talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high anchor and a still-disrupted strait — with US–Iran diplomacy (MoU to ~18-Aug) the swing factor.

Platts duty-unpaid last publicly marked ~$525–560 (mid $542.50, 03-Jul). The 6% EU duty, CBAM and Mozal's care & maintenance keep the structural floor; the war-risk freight component is easing at the margin as the US–Iran conflict de-escalates. The prospective 20% strait toll died on 14-Jul.

SMM's weekly DDP print ~109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul); Platts MW Transaction last printed 108.7¢ (03-Jul). The 50% Section-232 tariff-$ leg (≈$1,577 at the 17-Jul LME cash) dominates the all-in — US premia track Washington and the LME, not local tightness. Alcoa Q2 confirmed the tariff as a ~$35m q/q cost on its Canadian metal.

The decomposition below separates the mechanical tariff leg (Section-232 rate × LME) from the residual market premium. Most of the US 'premium' is a policy transfer, not a scarcity signal — and the new 20% strait toll extends the same logic to the freight leg of any Gulf-origin cargo, US-bound or not.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q3 settled $395 (decade high, unchanged); CIF spot ~$365 on squeeze risk under the LME's deep backwardation. The overnight escalation (9th consecutive night of strikes, further US casualties, Strait transits near a standstill) strengthens the case for Q4 talks (~Sep) to open at or above the $395 anchor rather than fade toward the lower half of the $340–410 range.

European (EDU): last public Platts mark ~$525–560 (03-Jul), unchanged. The 6% duty + CBAM floor plus Mozal's outage keep Europe structurally tighter than pre-war; the marginal driver — Gulf war-risk freight — continues to widen rather than ease.

US Midwest: SMM DDP ~109.75¢/lb (10-Jul), unchanged. The tariff leg (50% × LME ≈ $1,577 at the 17-Jul cash) is ~two-thirds of the all-in, so US premia track the LME and Washington more than Gulf events directly — but a sustained, further-escalating Gulf disruption raises input-cost and logistics risk for any US-bound metal that transits affected routes. Watch the next weekly print, not the spot survey.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply persists — Metro Mining shipped a record 1.8 Mt in Q2 (+7% y/y; 2026 guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held). Guinea is reportedly considering capping 2026 exports near 150 Mt (vs 178 Mt shipped in 2025, a ~15.7% cut) targeting >$100/dmt, but no formal decree had been issued as of end-June; no fresher public print surfaced over the weekend.

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst/ChemTradeAsia); no fresher public print over the weekend.

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent); no fresher public print over the weekend.

alumina_outlook: Alumina: surplus intact and deepening — EGA's restart ramps through H2, SHFE-adjacent SMM Alumina Index eased to ¥2,730.17/t (−¥12.8 w/w), and ex-China consolidation has stepped down to $300–330 (FOB Australia $328, API ~$303, Platts futures ~$308). SMM's June ex-China output dip (−6% y/y) and Guinea's still-undecreed quota push are the counterweights to watch.

Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38); record Australian shipments add length. Guinea is reportedly weighing a 2026 export cap near 150 Mt (vs 178 Mt in 2025) to defend a >$100/dmt target, but had issued no formal decree as of end-June.

Caustic soda / lime / freight: caustic stable (~$500/t FOB NE Asia). The delivered-cost leg has moved further the wrong way overnight — a 9th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran, a second consecutive strike on Kuwait's desalination/power infrastructure, and Strait transits down to as few as ~3 vessels/24h mean war-risk cover on Gulf-bound reagent and alumina freight is wider again, not fading. The commodity is still cheap; insuring and moving it through the Gulf is more expensive and less certain than at any point since the conflict began.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen. Energy complex firm: Brent broke above $90/bbl in early Monday trade (highest since mid-June) on the further-escalated US-Iran conflict; EU TTF gas ~€53/MWh — gas-indexed smelter power follows with a lag. Rising carbon-cost exposure.

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-transparent leg of the input basket — Cu 3M $13,606.00 (16-Jul official, latest published); the complex firmed through the week (Cu +8.8% YTD). Busbar/cathode exposure prices off the LME.

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.note: Overnight the US flew a 9th consecutive night of strikes on Iran and is reinforcing with F-16s (Germany) and F-35s (UK). Wire reporting puts Strait transits at as few as ~3 vessels per 24 hours, versus a normal ~110/day pre-war; the IRGC claims to have intercepted 4 further vessels. ~80–85% of Gulf aluminium exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports use this waterway.

logistics.name: Southern (Omani) corridor
logistics.val: Not verified reopened; risk further widened
logistics.note: The workaround corridor discussed in prior weeks has not materialised into confirmed reopening. With Oman among the states struck in recent days and the conflict escalating (9th night of strikes, mounting US casualties), the corridor's near-term viability looks weaker than at any point since it was first floated.

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.val: Sharply wider — escalation continuing, not fading
logistics.note: Lloyd's List and the Hormuz Strait Monitor have cited war-risk quotes around $10–14m per voyage on some routes, with rates near 2.5% and up to ~5% for US/UK/Israel-linked tonnage. With a 9th consecutive night of strikes, further US casualties, and repeated hits on Kuwaiti energy/water infrastructure, retain elevated insurance, interdiction and deviation-risk assumptions in all Gulf-routed freight and delivered-cost models.

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.val: 2,752 (17-Jul close, −3.1% d/d) — latest published, today's print pending
logistics.note: Last published at 2,752 on 17-Jul (−3.10% d/d, third straight down session), down more than 6% on the week — dry-bulk demand softening even as the Gulf war-risk premium (a separate, faster-moving cost line) continues to build. Today's session print was not yet available at this report's refresh; price the two legs (dry-bulk freight vs. war-risk surcharge) separately (Trading Economics/Hellenic Shipping News/Baltic Exchange).

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: With Strait transits near a standstill and the conflict escalating rather than cooling (9th night of strikes, further US casualties), overland trucking to Red Sea ports remains a more actively-used contingency than earlier in the conflict. Keep carrier capacity, costings and lead times current for any Gulf-origin cargo.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Repeated strikes on Kuwaiti and other Gulf infrastructure, plus mounting US casualties, keep force-majeure and deviation clauses firmly live for any Gulf-routed cargo; war-risk surcharge pass-through and interdiction/delay risk are live, worsening contract points to negotiate cargo-by-cargo. Generic supply-chain observation, not company-specific.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material and tradeable): power - Brent topped $90/bbl on Monday as the conflict intensified, with EU gas exposure following on a lag; calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency); silicon and magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated); copper (LME-transparent, 3M day-delayed close $13,525.50 on 20-Jul, -0.54%).

Easing for the first time since March: alumina. EGA's Al Taweelah refinery has been at full production since 10-Jul, targeting 50% of plant capacity within days and full capability by end-2026. At 2.4 Mt in 2025 it covered close to half of EGA's alumina requirement. This is the single most important input-cost development in the current picture.
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B - no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (roughly 85% China), CPC, and Gulf-routed material now exposed to enforced rather than merely threatened interdiction at Hormuz.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: LONG TERM (weekly / position). The count remains a completed five-wave impulse from the 2,300 base into the 3,855 high of 02-Jun-2026 - wave 5 extended, as commodities typically do - followed by a corrective A-B-C: A to 3,359, B to 3,406, C bottoming at 3,061.50 on 02-Jul, holding the .50 retracement at 3,077 within the 2,950-3,110 target zone (confluence of the .50 and the prior fourth of lesser degree). All three cardinal rules hold: wave 2 did not exceed 100% of wave 1, wave 3 is not the shortest, and wave 4 does not overlap wave 1. BASE CASE (55%): the C low is in and price is building a corrective base beneath the .382 trigger at 3,260; the firm 20-21 Jul action (official +$2 to 3,156, session ~3,167) is base-building, not a new leg down. A sustained break above 3,260 opens 3,450. BULL ALTERNATE (32%): a new impulse is already forming off 3,061.50 - a decisive close above 3,260 with the backwardation widening (now +$8.50) would confirm, targeting 3,620-3,800; the escalating Gulf tape and record-low stocks tilt risk this way. BEAR ALTERNATE (13%): a durable ceasefire releases the war premium and the C leg extends as a deeper zigzag toward the .618 at 2,894. INVALIDATION: a weekly close below 3,061.50 negates the completed-C reading; a weekly close above 3,406 (the B high) negates the bear case. CONFIDENCE: moderate - the .50 hold, the widening backwardation and the firm tape support the base-to-bull lean, but the 10-day ceasefire proposal argues for tight invalidation discipline. Technical context only, not advice.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: SHORT TERM (daily / swing). The corrective sequence off the 3,855 high of 02-Jun counts as A to 3,359, B to 3,607 (the spike that was sold), and C to 3,065 on 02-Jul, which held the .50 retracement. Since then price has carved a shallow base: the early-July low near 3,085, a rebound that stalled below the 3,230 MA20, and a firm 20-21 Jul (official 3,156, session ~3,167) that is pressing back toward resistance. Because wave A was a five-wave move, alternation argues the correction resolves as a zigzag rather than a flat, and the shallow, overlapping recovery is consistent with base-building. BASE CASE (55%): the base holds and price grinds toward the 3,230 MA20 and then the .382 at 3,260. BULL ALTERNATE (32%): a gap higher on a fresh escalation clears 3,260 directly - asymmetric given LME stocks at the lowest since 2022 and a widening backwardation. BEAR ALTERNATE (13%): a durable ceasefire and a failure back below 3,085 opens a retest of 3,061.50. INVALIDATION: a daily close below 3,061.50 voids the base-building count; a daily close above 3,260 voids the bear alternate. Watch RSI for divergence at the 3,230-3,260 stack and whether the cash-3M backwardation keeps steepening - curve confirmation is the tell. CONFIDENCE: moderate. Technical context only, not advice.

premium_drivers.t: Hormuz: enforcement, not just threat - the dominant driver
premium_drivers.d: Iran disabled two oil tankers attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz without permission on Monday 20-Jul, the first direct interdiction of individual merchant vessels in this conflict. A third US service member was killed since Friday, US strikes reached a ninth consecutive day, and Iran struck Kuwaiti power/desalination sites and Bahrain. With roughly 9% of global primary aluminium supply originating in the Gulf, war-risk cover and routing costs are building rather than fading.
src: NPR - U.S. and Iran launch new strikes after 3 U.S. service members are killed (20-Jul-2026)

premium_drivers.t: US Section 232 at 50% - the administered floor under the US duty-paid premium
premium_drivers.d: Products made entirely or almost entirely of aluminium pay 50% on their full customs value under the proclamation signed 01-Jun-2026, effective 08-Jun-2026 through 31-Dec-2027, with CBP guidance issued 05-Jun-2026 (CSMS #68855869). At an LME around $3,150/t this is roughly $1,575/t of pure policy cost embedded in US duty-paid metal, mechanically decoupling the US premium from the rest of the world.
src: U.S. Customs and Border Protection - Section 232 Tariffs on Steel and Aluminum FAQs

premium_drivers.t: Europe: war premium fading against a structural floor
premium_drivers.d: Platts assessed duty-unpaid P1020A in-warehouse Rotterdam at $525-560/t (mid $542.50) on 03-Jul, down from $600-625/t on 13-May as the Middle East war premium receded. The floor beneath it is structural rather than sentiment-driven: the 6% EU import duty, CBAM, and idled European capacity. Liquidity remains thin as Rotterdam competes with Asia for units.
src: S&P Global - TRADE REVIEW: European aluminum market seeks floor as Middle East war premium fades (07-Jul-2026)

premium_drivers.t: Japan: Q3 settled at a decade high, Q4 negotiations open against it
premium_drivers.d: The Q3-2026 MJP CIF Japan premium settled at $395/t (assessed 06-Jul), a decade high and roughly 12% above Q2's $350-353/t. Q4 talks open around September against that anchor; the swing variable is whether Hormuz disruption persists at current intensity or a diplomatic track restores flow.
src: S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP settlement $395/t, via IndexBox

premium_settlement_src: S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP settlement $395/t (assessed 06-Jul-2026), via IndexBox

so_what.line: The US-Iran conflict entered an 11th night of strikes and Iran hit another tanker in the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, while Yemen's Houthis declared a maritime embargo on Saudi Arabia - a potential new front - even as mediators floated a 10-day ceasefire; aluminium still firmed, the 21-Jul session trading to about $3,167/t.
points: What moved: the 21-Jul session traded aluminium up ~0.9% to about $3,167/t (Trading Economics), and the latest LME official (20-Jul, published overnight) firmed to cash $3,156.00 / 3M $3,147.50, with the cash-3M backwardation widening to +$8.50 as stock fell to 279,775 t. The 21-Jul official had not yet posted by Westmetall at this ~06:05 London refresh.

Why it matters: an 11th night of US strikes, a fresh Hormuz tanker attack, and a Houthi embargo threat against Saudi shipping keep roughly 9% of global primary supply at risk with strait traffic largely stalled - the shipping risk now sits directly on the physical supply line.

The two-sided catalyst: mediators put a 10-day ceasefire proposal in front of Washington and Tehran. A durable truce would release the war-risk premium and reopen Gulf shipping; a collapse (like the June MoU) would re-escalate. Either way it is now the swing variable.

The offset and the watch: EGA's Al Taweelah alumina restart and record Chinese exports (June 711 kt) keep the flat price rangebound. Watch Norsk Hydro's Q2 out this morning (22-Jul), whether LME stock keeps drawing below 279,775 t, and whether the backwardation widens on the next official.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
